title: Chapter II: Rights and Entitlements
subtitle: Training module (pilot) for the RPwD Act 2016 Easy Read Guide. For trainers and support workers. Full 14 segments. ~20 minutes.
audience: DPO staff, community rehabilitation workers, teachers, social workers, family carers preparing to teach the Act

segments.title: Learning objectives

slides.html:
By the end of this module, a learner can:

  1. List the 13 rights protected under Chapter II of the RPwD Act 2016, grouped into three families: equality, protection, and autonomy.
  2. Cite the correct Section number when a client tells them a Chapter II right has been broken.
  3. Explain, in one plain sentence a client can follow, the difference between equality (S3), community life (S5), and legal capacity (S13).
  4. Choose between Chapter II and other chapters (III, IV, V) when a client's issue could sit in more than one place.

Verbs picked from Bloom's revised taxonomy: remember, understand, apply, analyse.

segments.html:
By the end of this module, a learner can:

  1. List the 13 rights protected under Chapter II of the RPwD Act 2016, grouped into three families: equality, protection, and autonomy.
  2. Cite the correct Section number when a client tells them a Chapter II right has been broken.
  3. Explain, in one plain sentence a client can follow, the difference between equality (S3), community life (S5), and legal capacity (S13).
  4. Choose between Chapter II and other chapters (III, IV, V) when a client's issue could sit in more than one place.

Verbs picked from Bloom's revised taxonomy: remember, understand, apply, analyse.

segments.title: Why this matters for your practice

slides.html:
Chapter II is the chapter you will cite most often. Most complaints a support worker writes on behalf of a client will name at least one Section from S3 to S15.

Get the right Section number on the complaint, and the officer receiving it has to act. Get it wrong, and the complaint gets passed around until the client gives up. Trainers who confuse S3 (equality, the ceiling) with S13 (legal capacity, the operative right in bank account and property cases) lose cases that should have been open and shut.

This module trains you to pick the right Section fast, and to teach that skill to the workers you supervise.

segments.title: Entry scenario

slides.html:
Reshma is a support worker at a DPO in Chennai. Her client Karthik is 34 years old and has an intellectual disability. Karthik lives with his mother. Karthik's uncle has walked into the branch of Karthik's bank and asked staff to transfer the joint account to his own name, saying, "the boy cannot sign properly, I will manage the money."

The branch manager has told the uncle to come back with a doctor's letter. The uncle is coming back on Friday. Today is Wednesday. Reshma has one meeting with the family this evening.

Which Section of the RPwD Act 2016 should Reshma cite first when she writes to the branch manager tomorrow morning?

Hold your answer. We will come back to this case in segment 8.

Hold your answer. We will come back to this case in segment 8.

segments.title: Prior-knowledge activation

slides.html:
Before we open Chapter II, one quick question to bring your memory of the Act into the room.

Which chapter of the RPwD Act 2016 contains the Rights and Entitlements sections?

a. Chapter I
b. Chapter II
c. Chapter III
d. Chapter V

Show answer

b. Chapter II.

Chapter I is Preliminary (definitions). Chapter II is Rights and Entitlements (S3 to S15), which is what this module covers. Chapter III is Education. Chapter V is Social Security, Health, Rehabilitation and Recreation.

If you picked wrong: no problem, that is what this segment is for. Read on.

segments.html:
Before we open Chapter II, one quick question to bring your memory of the Act into the room.

Which chapter of the RPwD Act 2016 contains the Rights and Entitlements sections?

a. Chapter I
b. Chapter II
c. Chapter III
d. Chapter V

Show answer

b. Chapter II.

Chapter I is Preliminary (definitions). Chapter II is Rights and Entitlements (S3 to S15), which is what this module covers. Chapter III is Education. Chapter V is Social Security, Health, Rehabilitation and Recreation.

If you picked wrong: no problem, that is what this segment is for. Read on.

segments.title: Concept segment 1: The three families of Chapter II

slides.html:
Chapter II covers 13 sections. Trying to memorise 13 separate rights is hard. Group them into three families and the shape holds.

Family 1: Equality and non-discrimination (S3, S4)

  • S3 Equality and non-discrimination. Every person with a disability has the right to be treated equally. No one can treat a person badly because of their disability.
  • S4 Women and children with disabilities. Women and children with disabilities have the same rights as everyone else. The government must protect them from harm.

Family 1 is the ceiling. It states the principle. On its own it is broad; you will almost always cite it alongside a more specific Section from Family 2 or 3.

Family 2: Protection and safety (S6, S7, S8)

  • S6 Protection from cruelty and inhuman treatment.
  • S7 Protection from abuse, violence and exploitation.
  • S8 Protection and safety. In disasters, fires, floods, and armed conflict, the government must keep persons with disabilities safe.

Family 2 covers what has already happened, or what is about to happen. Cite these when the client is unsafe now.

Family 3: Autonomy: home, family, decisions (S5, S9 to S15)

  • S5 Community life. The right to live in your own home and community. No one can force a person into an institution.
  • S9 Home and family. The right to a home and a family. No child can be taken from a parent only because the parent has a disability.
  • S10 Reproductive rights. The right to decide about marriage and children.
  • S11 Accessibility in voting. The right to vote in private, with accessible polling stations.
  • S12 Access to justice. The right to go to court, with sign language interpretation and reasonable accommodation.
  • S13 Legal capacity. The right to make your own decisions, own things, run a bank account. Legal capacity cannot be taken away because of a disability.
  • S14 Provision for guardianship. Guardianship can only be a last step, limited, and ended when the person can decide for themselves.
  • S15 Designation of authorities to support. The government must name offices that help persons with disabilities make their own decisions.

Family 3 is the largest and the newest in Indian law. Cases about bank accounts, property, marriage, and forced institutionalisation land here.

One idea to take away: when a client tells you a story, first ask which family it belongs to. Then pick the Section.

Equality and Protection and Autonomy. Three questions in order. Ask them of every case that walks in.

segments.title: Inline knowledge check

slides.html:
A client with a psychosocial disability is refused a savings account by branch staff. They say she is "unable to sign". Which Section applies?

a. Section 3, Equality
b. Section 7, Abuse and violence
c. Section 13, Legal capacity
d. Section 15, Authorities to support

Show answer and feedback

c. Section 13, Legal capacity.

  • a. Section 3 (Equality) is true but broad. Cite it as backup.
  • b. Section 7 (Abuse and violence) belongs to a different family. S7 covers physical or sexual violence. Refusing a service falls under legal capacity (S13).
  • c. Section 13 (Legal capacity) is the operative right. S13 says a person cannot be denied legal capacity, including running a bank account, because of a disability. This is the citation the branch manager has to respond to.
  • d. Section 15 (Authorities to support) points to the office that helps the client claim S13. Useful as the next step, after S13.

Rule of thumb: the operative Section is the one that names the specific right that has been broken. Family 1 goes on top as backup.

segments.html:

b. Section 7, Abuse and violence
c. Section 13, Legal capacity

Show answer and feedback

c. Section 13, Legal capacity.

  • a. Section 3 (Equality) is true but broad. Cite it as backup.
  • b. Section 7 (Abuse and violence) belongs to a different family. S7 covers physical or sexual violence. Refusing a service falls under legal capacity (S13).
  • c. Section 13 (Legal capacity) is the operative right. S13 says a person cannot be denied legal capacity, including running a bank account, because of a disability. This is the citation the branch manager has to respond to.
  • d. Section 15 (Authorities to support) points to the office that helps the client claim S13. Useful as the next step, after S13.

Rule of thumb: the operative Section is the one that names the specific right that has been broken. Family 1 goes on top as backup.

segments.title: Concept segment 2: The picking table

slides.html:
Segment 5 gave you the families. Segment 7 gives you the picking table. Same content, different form. Print this and keep it beside your desk.

The client's situation The Section to cite first Family
Refused a service, a job, or a place because of a disability S3 Equality
Woman or child at risk of harm S4 Equality
Community says she must move to an institution S5 Autonomy
Threatened, beaten, or being restrained cruelly S6 Protection
Sexually or physically abused, or used for someone's gain S7 Protection
Left behind in a flood, fire, or during a crisis S8 Protection
A child is being taken from a parent with a disability S9 Autonomy
Family or hospital wants to force sterilisation, or block a marriage S10 Autonomy
Cannot vote in private, or ballot is not accessible S11 Autonomy
Court will not let a client take part, or refuses an interpreter S12 Autonomy
Bank, property, or contract refused because "she cannot decide" S13 Autonomy
A family member has taken full guardianship over a competent adult S14 Autonomy
Client needs support to make a decision, no one is available S15 Autonomy

Reading the table: the Section on the left is the operative citation. Add S3 or S4 as the backup ceiling. Add S15 as the next step, since S15 is the office that helps the client claim any of these rights.

Alt-form note for the app: the online version renders this table with band-tint row backgrounds so a screen-reader user hears "Section 13, Autonomy family" as row context, and a low-vision user can scan by colour and Section number together. Colour is never the only signal.

segments.title: Worked example: Reshma and Karthik's bank account

slides.html:
Back to the scenario in segment 3. Here is how Reshma works the case step by step.

Step 1. Name the operative right. Karthik has a right to run his own bank account. That is Section 13, Legal capacity. Reshma writes down "S13" at the top of her notepad.

Step 2. Add the ceiling. Denying the account because Karthik has an intellectual disability is discrimination. Reshma adds "S3" beside "S13".

Step 3. Check for guardianship danger. The uncle has said "I will manage the money". That is a guardianship claim. Section 14 says guardianship can only be a last step, limited, and only when the person cannot decide even with support. Karthik can decide with support. Reshma adds "S14" as the guard-rail.

Step 4. Name the support Karthik is entitled to. Section 15 says the government must name offices that help persons with disabilities make their own decisions. Reshma will ask the District Social Welfare Officer to name the supporter for Karthik. She adds "S15".

Step 5. Write the complaint. One page, three paragraphs. Reshma uses this template.

To the Branch Manager, [Bank name], [Branch address]

I am writing on behalf of Mr Karthik [surname], account holder number [xxxx], and with his consent. On [date], my client's uncle asked the branch to transfer this account to his name on the grounds that my client "cannot sign properly". This request should be refused.

My client has a right to run his own bank account. This is Section 13 of the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act 2016, which protects legal capacity. Denying this right because of a disability is also a breach of Section 3, Equality. Guardianship under Section 14 of the same Act can only be a last step, limited in scope, and ordered by a competent authority. It cannot be arranged over a counter conversation.

If my client needs support to sign or to understand the transaction, the branch can request a supporter under Section 15. My office and the District Social Welfare Officer can arrange this. Please confirm in writing that the account will remain in my client's name and that the request has been refused.

[Signature, name, DPO, phone]

Step 6. Copy in. Copy the District Social Welfare Officer, the State Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities (S79), and Karthik's mother.

Step 7. Follow up in 48 hours. In writing. Reshma marks Friday morning on her calendar.

Notice the pattern: one operative Section, one ceiling, one guard-rail, one next-step Section. Four numbers, one page, one client kept safe.

segments.title: Case with a decision point

slides.html:
Priya is a support worker at a small DPO in a district town. Her client Lakshmi is 29, has a psychosocial disability, and has been married for six years. Lakshmi tells Priya, in private, that her husband beats her when she has a bad week, and last month he said, "next time, I will send you to the mental hospital and leave you there." Lakshmi has bruises on her arm. She wants Priya's help. She does not want to go to the police station herself. She has one child, aged three.

Priya can walk to the Special Court that afternoon. She has one hour with Lakshmi first.

Which Section should Priya cite first in the complaint she drafts?

a. Section 3, Equality and non-discrimination.
b. Section 6, Protection from cruelty and inhuman treatment.
c. Section 7, Protection from abuse, violence and exploitation.
d. Section 9, Home and family.

Show feedback per option

a. Section 3, Equality. Weak first citation. Equality is the ceiling and belongs in the letter, at the top of the citations block, as backup. It does not name what is happening to Lakshmi. Pick again.

b. Section 6, Protection from cruelty and inhuman treatment. Reasonable, and worth citing. S6 covers cruel treatment. If the beating is the only fact, S6 alone can carry the complaint. In this case, other facts (repeated beatings, the threat of institutionalisation) push the operative citation further.

c. Section 7, Protection from abuse, violence and exploitation. This is the strongest first citation. S7 covers physical abuse and repeated violence, and it reads across to domestic settings. Cite S7 as the operative right, S6 as the accompanying protection right, S5 (community life, cannot force into an institution) because of the husband's threat, and S3 as the ceiling. Also cite S4 (women with disabilities) because Lakshmi is a woman, and this is where the RPwD Act specifically names extra government duty. Priya has a five-citation complaint that any Special Court officer will act on.

d. Section 9, Home and family. Cite this as a supporting Section. S9 protects the right to have a home and family and stops children being taken from a parent with a disability. It applies here because Lakshmi has a three-year-old and the household is in crisis. The operative right the husband has broken sits in S7.

Best answer: c. Second best: b. Third: d. Weakest: a.

Priya also does two things outside the Act citations. She calls a shelter to hold a room, and she gives Lakshmi the number of a woman-only DPO helpline. Section citations anchor the paper trail that follows. The shelter room and helpline hold Lakshmi's safety while that paper trail moves.

segments.title: Reflection

slides.html:
Take five minutes with a pen. Answer for yourself, in your own notebook.

What? Think of one case from the last year in which you saw a Chapter II right broken. Do not name the client. Name the situation in one sentence.

So what? Which Section number would you now cite that you did not cite then, or did not know about then? Write the number.

Now what? Which one person on your team most needs to hear this Section number next week? Write their name and the day you will tell them.

You are the person who carries the citation forward from this room to the next case. If the answer to "now what?" is nobody, then this module has not landed. Sit with the reflection a while longer.

segments.title: Application task for your setting

slides.html:
This week. Pull one open case file, complaint draft, or note from the last three months. Read it once. At the top of the page, write the Chapter II Section number that applies. If more than one applies, write the operative Section, then the ceiling, then the guard-rail. Bring the case file to your next team meeting and ask the room whether they would have picked the same numbers.

Next module. Bring the disagreements. Chapter III (Education) covers school and college; Chapter IV (Skill Development and Employment) covers jobs; Chapter V (Social Security, Health, Rehabilitation and Recreation) covers pensions and healthcare. If your case sits across two chapters, note which one and which Section. This is where the picking gets harder, and where the next modules will pick up.

segments.title: Discussion prompt

slides.html:
Only shown in the app when learners have opted into the trainer network. Held for v2.2 in this pilot.

In one sentence: which Chapter II Section do most of your clients not know they have? Post one sentence. Read three others before you leave.

Reason for holding: the trainer network channel goes live later. Once it does, this segment lights up.

segments.title: Further reading

slides.html:
Two Act sources, two commentary sources. Each with year and offline flag.

  1. The RPwD Act 2016, Chapter II, full text. Ministry of Law and Justice, 2016. Available in this app under Sections 3 to 15, verified against the Gazette text. Offline: yes, once the app is installed. 1
  2. The RPwD Rules 2017, Ministry of Social Justice and Empowerment. Rules under the Act that specify how S15 (support authorities) is meant to work in practice. Offline: download the PDF from the Ministry site once, keep it in your DPO shared folder. 2
  3. The Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities annual reports (Chief Commissioner and each State Commissioner). These reports name the actual complaints filed under Chapter II each year and how they were resolved. Offline: download the state report for your state once a year. 3
  4. Supported Decision Making in India: a practitioner guide. For trainers who want to go deeper on S13 to S15. Any recent practitioner guide from a DPO federation or legal-policy centre works. Offline: keep the PDF you use. 4

Endnotes are at the bottom of this file. Every claim from outside the Act sits behind a numbered endnote. If a source cannot be verified and dated, cut it. Do not cite from memory.

segments.title: Mark complete

slides.html:
When the learner reaches this point in the app, they can tick "I have finished this module." The tick is stored in localStorage under a per-module key. Clearing browser storage clears the tick and only the tick. No account, no email, no server call.

Trainer note: mark-complete is a personal marker for the trainer's own use. It helps them see, at the top of the Training Resources page, which of the 17 chapters they have already worked through.

segments.html:
When the learner reaches this point in the app, they can tick "I have finished this module." The tick is stored in localStorage under a per-module key. Clearing browser storage clears the tick and only the tick. No account, no email, no server call.

Trainer note: mark-complete is a personal marker for the trainer's own use. It helps them see, at the top of the Training Resources page, which of the 17 chapters they have already worked through.

takeaway_html:
One-page summary for printing (this is the handout the app renders as a PDF; the deck is generated from the same source).

  • Chapter II is Sections 3 to 15. Thirteen rights, three families: Equality (S3, S4), Protection (S6, S7, S8), Autonomy (S5, S9 to S15).
  • The picking table (segment 7) sits on your desk.
  • One operative Section, one ceiling (S3 or S4), one guard-rail (S14 for guardianship, S15 for support), one next step.
  • Complaints without a Section number get passed around. Complaints with the right number get answered.
  • S13, Legal capacity, is the most under-used Section. Bank, property, contract, and consent cases live here.
  • S7, Abuse and violence, is the strongest first citation when a client is being harmed at home.
endnotes_html:
1. The Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act 2016, Chapter II Sections 3 to 15. Government of India, Ministry of Law and Justice, published in The Gazette of India, 2016. The full text used by this app is verified against the Gazette. Available offline once the app is installed to the home screen. Back

  2. The Rights of Persons with Disabilities Rules 2017, Government of India, Ministry of Social Justice and Empowerment, notified 15 June 2017. The Rules elaborate how Sections 15, 20, 31, 42, and others work in practice. The trainer keeps a downloaded copy in the DPO shared folder for offline use. Back

  3. Annual Reports of the Chief Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities (Union) and the State Commissioners for Persons with Disabilities (per state), Government of India and State Governments, 2017 onwards. These name Chapter II complaints filed and their resolution. The trainer downloads the report for their state once a year and keeps it offline. Back

  4. Practitioner guide on Supported Decision Making in India (S13 to S15 in practice). The trainer picks a recent guide from a DPO federation or legal-policy centre they trust, verifies the year, and keeps the PDF offline. If no verified guide is available, cite the Act alone and cut this endnote from the module. Back
